relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Abraham was old and blessed in everything, and the promise now needed to pass forward.
He called the elder servant of his house into a solemn oath.
The wife for Isaac was not to be taken from the surrounding Canaanite daughters.
The servant was sent back toward Abraham’s kin to seek a woman for Isaac.
The servant asked the key question: what if the woman will not come?
Abraham refused to send Isaac backward.
He trusted that the Eternal would send a messenger ahead and guide the way.
If the woman would not come, the servant would be free from the oath.
The oath was sealed.
The servant set out with camels and gifts toward the city of Nahor.
At evening, he stopped by the well, where women came to draw water.
He prayed for the Eternal’s covenant-kindness to meet him in the moment.
He stood by the spring as the daughters of the city came out.
He asked for a sign of generosity: the one who would offer water beyond what was requested.
Before the prayer was finished, Rebekah came out with her jar.
She was beautiful, untouched, and active, going down to the spring and coming up with water.
The servant ran to meet her and asked for a little water.
She answered quickly: Drink, my lord.
Then she offered water for the camels too, fulfilling the sign through abundant care.
She ran and drew until all the camels were watered.
The servant watched in silence, discerning whether the Eternal had made the journey successful.
When the camels finished, he gave her gold.
He asked her family and whether there was room to stay.
She named herself as kin of Nahor through Bethuel and Milcah.
She offered abundance again: straw, feed, and lodging.
The servant bowed and worshiped.
He blessed the Eternal for covenant-kindness, truth, and guidance on the way.
Rebekah ran to her mother’s house with the news.
Laban entered the story and ran to the spring.
Seeing the gifts and hearing Rebekah’s words, he came to the man at the camels.
He welcomed the blessed one of the Eternal into the prepared house.
Hospitality was given to servant, men, and camels.
But the servant would not eat until he had spoken his mission.
He identified himself as Abraham’s servant.
He testified to Abraham’s blessing and abundance.
He told of Sarah’s impossible son and Isaac’s inheritance.
He repeated Abraham’s oath concerning Isaac’s wife.
The woman must come from Abraham’s family line.
Again the question of her willingness was named.
Abraham had trusted the Eternal’s messenger to make the way successful.
If the family refused, the servant would be released.
The servant recounted his prayer at the spring.
He described the sign he had asked for.
The appointed woman would offer water for both servant and camels.
Before his heart had finished speaking, Rebekah appeared.
She acted exactly according to the sign.
Her lineage was confirmed, and the gifts were placed upon her.
The servant worshiped, recognizing the true way unfolding.
He asked the family to answer with covenant-kindness and truth.
Laban and Bethuel recognized that the matter had gone out from the Eternal.
Rebekah was placed before the servant as the one spoken by the Eternal.
The servant bowed to the earth before the Eternal.
Gifts were given to Rebekah and to her family.
After the night, the servant asked to return to his master.
Her family wanted delay.
The servant pressed not to delay the successful way.
They called Rebekah and asked her own mouth.
Rebekah answered clearly: I will go.
They sent Rebekah, her nurse, and the servant away.
They blessed her with multiplication and strength at the gate.
Rebekah rose with her young women and followed the path toward Isaac.
Isaac was near Beer-lahai-roi, the well of the Living One who sees.
He went out to meditate in the field at evening and saw the camels coming.
Rebekah lifted her eyes and saw Isaac.
She asked who he was, then covered herself with the veil.
The servant told Isaac all that had happened.
Isaac brought Rebekah into Sarah’s tent, loved her, and was comforted after his mother.
